title: Hypericum debile
taxonomy: Hypericum debile is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Hypericaceae, commonly known as the St. John's Wort family. This family is native to the Old World, with many species found in Africa, Asia, and Europe. The genus Hypericum contains over 500 species, with Hypericum debile being one of them. It is further classified as a member of the order Malpighiales, which includes a diverse range of flowering plants. The exact classification of Hypericum debile can be further broken down into its binomial nomenclature, but it is generally classified as Hypericum debile (Hook.f. & Thomson) Hook.f.

parts: Hypericum debile is not a well-documented plant, and information on its medicinal parts is limited. The parts of Hypericum plants that are commonly used for medicinal purposes include the leaves, flowers, and stems. intro: Hypericum debile, also known as slender St. John's Wort, is a perennial herb native to North America, belonging to the Hypericum genus and characterized by its yellow flowers and slender stems. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral properties, which are said to aid in wound healing, reduce anxiety and depression, and alleviate symptoms of herpes and other viral infections. The bioactive constituents of Hypericum debile include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and hyperforin, a lignan with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant are teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat anxiety, insomnia, and skin conditions, and to promote wound healing and immune system function.
